nodes.body: Nia missed the last bus home, and the doors of the Windvale Museum of Clockwork Animals were locked tight for the night. She was shut inside until morning. All around her stood brass birds and tin lions and copper fish, hundreds of clockwork creatures wound down long ago. Every one of them had gone still and silent. On her shoulder rode Tock, her tiny wind-up mouse, ticking softly as if to say come on, come on. Four great wings opened off the dark entrance hall. There was the brass aviary, the tin savanna, the copper deep-sea hall, and the crystal insect conservatory. Her candle stub was already burning low. Nia had to pick just one to explore before the little flame went out.

choices.label: Slip into the copper deep-sea hall.

nodes.body: Nia stepped into the brass aviary, and it took her breath away. Hundreds of clockwork birds sat frozen on their perches, their beaks still open on songs that had stopped hours before. Nothing moved. Nothing sang. The whole huge room hung in a hush so deep she could hear her own heart. On her shoulder Tock ticked and ticked, quick and eager, nudging her ear as if to say there was no time to waste. Nia nodded, held her candle a little higher, and walked in under the silent birds.

choices.label: Walk in under the silent birds.

nodes.body: A little way in, the aviary split into two paths. Straight ahead a wide floor of tall perch-trees led deeper among the biggest birds, brass hawks and herons standing still as statues. To one side, a narrow spiral stair wound up and up toward the high nesting boxes near the dark glass roof. The stair looked old and thin. The floor looked broad and safe. Tock ran a nervous circle on her shoulder, unsure which way to send her. Nia looked from the quiet trees to the climbing stair and made up her mind.

choices.label: Climb the spiral stair to the nests.

nodes.body: Among the perch-trees the aviary felt like a still brass forest, branch after branch crowded with sleeping birds. Nia trod softly so her footsteps would not echo. Two ways opened between the trunks. On one side lay the songbird alcove, a cosy nook packed with tiny larks and wrens no bigger than her thumb. On the other side stood the great raptor roost, where a huge brass eagle spread its still wings above a watchful bronze owl. Tock looked one way, then the other. Nia weighed the small singers against the great hunters and chose where to step.

The songbird alcove was a whole wall of tiny brass larks and wrens, each one no bigger than Nia's thumb. They perched in neat rows, wings folded, heads tilted, as if they had all fallen asleep in the middle of a chorus. She leaned close and saw the little keys in their backs, every one wound down to nothing at all. Not a single bird could stir. Tock pressed his paws to the glass and gazed up at them, and Nia felt a soft tug of sadness for the silent flock. Then she went on.

Tock did not wait for her. He scampered ahead and stopped at a dusty music-box set into the wall, tapping it with one paw until Nia came to look. It was a grand old thing, its brass drum studded with hundreds of tiny pins. A faded label told her what it once did. Long ago this box had played every bird in the alcove at once, a whole singing wall, if only it still had power to turn. Nia ran her finger over the cold pins and wished she could hear it. Then Tock tugged her on, deeper still.

Behind the music-box, half hidden in shadow, a small brass card was pinned to the wall. Nia held her candle close and read the neat old writing there. It spoke of one special key, a master key, that could wake the whole museum at once. The card said it hung in the central rotunda, the great round hall at the very heart of the building. Nia's eyes went wide. If that was true, then one key might set every bird singing and every beast moving again. She traced the little map beneath the words and promised herself she would find it.

Nia tucked the clue safe in her pocket. Then she let Tock lead the way. He hopped down from her shoulder and darted off. A narrow keeper walkway ran behind the glass cases, out of sight of the day visitors. Nia followed. She ducked under low pipes and stepped over coils of old wire. The walkway smelled of oil and dust. It bent and turned, but it always led one way, toward the middle of the great building. She kept her candle steady and her feet careful. Ahead of her, Tock's little ticking shape trotted on through the dark.

The keeper walkway ended at a low service door. On it, painted in fading white, was the picture of a great clock, its hands frozen at some long-ago hour. Nia rested her palm flat on the wood and felt it thrum. Somewhere just beyond, the heart of the museum was humming, faint and deep and warm, like a giant asleep. Tock stood up on his back legs and quivered with excitement. This was it, the way to the middle. Nia took a slow breath to steady herself, wrapped her hand around the cold handle, and got ready to step forward.

The raptor roost was a place of shadows and great still wings. Here the big hunting birds waited on tall perches, far above the little singers. A brass eagle spread wings as wide as Nia was tall, every feather sharp and gleaming. Beside it a bronze owl sat round and heavy, its glassy eyes fixed on her and never blinking. She knew they were only clockwork. Still, her skin prickled, and she kept her steps slow and quiet. Tock huddled close against her neck. Together, girl and mouse, they moved along beneath the watching birds and looked for a way on.

On the floor beneath the eagle lay a single long feather, and Nia's heart leapt. It was shaped almost exactly like a key, thin and notched along one edge. She crouched and lifted it with careful fingers, sure she had found something wonderful. But it was too light. Turning it in the candlelight, she saw it was only a pretty brass decoy, a bit of art, with no true teeth to open any lock. She let out a small sigh and set it gently down again. Not the real thing. She would have to keep looking elsewhere.

The bronze owl, though, was doing something odd. While all the other birds sat square and even, the owl had one stiff wing held straight out to the side, pointing toward a low archway in the far wall. Nia studied it. The angle was too neat to be chance. Someone, some keeper long ago, must have set that wing on purpose, like a signpost for anyone who knew to look. Tock chirped and pointed his nose the same way. Nia grinned. If the old owl was showing her a path, then she would trust it and follow where it aimed.

Nia followed exactly where the owl's wing pointed. Through the low archway a short keeper stair dropped down into the dark, its steps worn smooth by years of quiet feet. She went down them one at a time, keeping one hand on the cool wall. With every step the warm hum grew louder and closer, that deep steady thrum she now knew came from the center of the whole museum. Tock rode her shoulder and ticked in time with it. At the bottom the air felt warmer, and a soft glow leaked around a doorway just ahead of her.

Both ways through the aviary brought Nia to the same place at last, the tall inner door that opened onto the central rotunda. It stood a little ajar, a line of pale grey light spilling through the gap. She could feel the great clock's hum right through the floor now. Before she could even reach for the handle, Tock leapt from her shoulder, squeezed through the narrow opening, and vanished into the room beyond with an eager squeak. Nia laughed under her breath. Trust the little mouse to go first. She set her hand to the door and pushed it wider.

The door swung slowly open, and the round hall lay before her. High overhead a great skylight let in the first grey light of dawn. It was soft and cold and silvery. It fell across a wide floor laid in a huge circle. A hundred years of visitors' feet had worn it smooth. This was it. Nia knew it at once, the true middle of the whole clockwork museum, the place every hall reached toward. She stepped inside, and her footsteps whispered around the curved walls. Tock waited in the very center. He sat up tall and ticked with pride, as if he had brought her home.

There, rising in the center of the rotunda, stood the great museum clock. It towered far above her head, its huge face pale in the dawn light, its long hands stopped and still. Around its base ran a ring of little hooks and shelves, the kind a keeper might use to hang tools. Nia's heart began to thump. The clue had promised a master key here, near the heart of everything, and this was surely the heart. Somewhere close by, she was certain, the keys that could wake the whole menagerie must be hanging and waiting. She hurried forward into the rotunda to find them.

Nia set her foot on the spiral stair and began to climb toward the high nests. It was a rickety, rusted thing, and it swayed a little under her weight. Up and up it curled toward the dark glass roof. She was halfway up when one thin rung gave a sharp warning crack beneath her shoe. She froze. The whole stair trembled underneath her. Her heart hammered against her ribs, and Tock clung tight to her collar. Below her the safe floor waited. Above her the shadowed nests were still so far away. Nia had to decide, and quickly, exactly what she should do.

choices.label: Grab the rail and climb back down.
Climb on toward the high nests.

nodes.body: Nia did the sensible thing. She stopped at once and did not take another step up. Both hands closed hard around the cold rail, and she held on and waited, breathing slow, while the shaking stair went still beneath her. Her heart pounded, but she did not panic. When everything had settled and gone quiet again, she began to climb back down, testing each rung with a careful toe before she trusted it. One step, then the next, then the next. At last her feet touched the solid aviary floor, and she could have hugged it with relief.

Safe on the floor again, Nia let out one long, shaky breath. Her legs still felt wobbly, and her hands ached from gripping the rail so hard. She looked back up at the high nests, small and dark near the roof, and shook her head. Whatever waited up there, it was not worth a fall in the dark. Some things were better left alone. She gave Tock a little pat to tell him she was fine, then turned away from the broken stair and set off instead toward the safer path among the tall perch-trees.

Nia should have gone back, but she climbed on anyway. The very last rung folded flat beneath her shoe, and the whole stair peeled slowly away from the wall with a long groan. She scrambled onto a narrow ledge among the sleeping birds and clung there, safe but stuck fast. There was no way up and no way down. Far below, Tock ran in worried circles, ticking hard. Nia sat very still and made herself calm. She was not hurt, only stranded. She would simply have to wave to Tock and wait up here until the morning staff came with a ladder to fetch her down.

The tin savanna opened wide and golden before her, and Nia stopped to stare. A whole plain of tin grass stretched away under the dim lights, painted the warm color of a sunset. A great lion stood caught mid-roar, its tin jaws frozen open. Giraffes rose as tall as the ceiling, their long necks lost in shadow. And far out in the middle distance loomed a huge elephant, dark and heavy and still. Nothing stirred in all that painted grass. Tock ticked softly by her ear. Nia squared her shoulders and stepped out onto the wide tin plain.

choices.label: Step out onto the tin plain.

nodes.body: Out on the savanna floor, Nia saw two ways she might go. Straight ahead the open plain rolled on toward the big animals, the lion and the giraffes and the great far elephant, with nothing between her and them but tin grass. To one side ran a narrow walkway that skirted the very edge of the hall, tucked against the painted horizon wall where the sky met the plain. The open floor felt bold. The edge walkway felt sly and quick. Tock's whiskers twitched this way and that. Nia thought it over and picked her path.

Out on the open floor the tin grass brushed cool against Nia's knees. The big animals loomed close on every side. Two of them drew her most. Off to the left the elephant herd gathered around a wide tin watering hole. They were gray shapes, standing patient in the dim. Off to the right rose the giraffe grove. Its tall spotted necks climbed up and up into the roof shadows. Tock could not choose for her. Nia looked from the heavy elephants to the towering giraffes, and she decided which wonder to walk toward first.

Up close, the great tin elephant was even bigger than Nia had guessed, a mountain of riveted metal towering over her. She crept near, craning to see its wrinkled tin ears and long curling tusks. Then, without warning, one last breath of leftover wind stirred inside it. Its trunk twitched. One huge foot scraped forward across the floor with a screech. Nia gasped and jumped back, her heart slamming. Tock squeaked in alarm. For a moment she was sure the beast would wake and come at her, and she stood frozen, staring up at the swaying giant.

The elephant lurched a slow half-step, groaning deep inside, and Nia's breath caught in her throat. It was not truly awake, she saw, but it was not fully asleep either. The last of its old wind still churned somewhere in its great chest, and now and then it moved. That made it dangerous, not out of anger, but simply because it was so huge and could not see her down there. She understood at once. She must give this half-wound giant a wide and careful berth, and never let herself stand where a stray foot might come down.

Moving slow and low, Nia ducked in behind a tall tin baobab tree and pressed herself against its cool trunk. From there she watched the elephant and waited. She did not rush. She did not try to slip past while it still swayed. Little by little the great beast wound down again, its groaning fading, its huge feet going quiet, until with one last long mechanical sigh it settled and stood perfectly still. Only then did Nia let her shoulders drop. Tock peeked out from her collar. The danger had passed, and patience had kept them both safe.

Safe past the sleeping elephant, Nia looked for a way on. Tock found it first. In the tin grass ran a faint worn trail. It was a line of little dark oil-drips, left behind over many years. A keeper trail, she realized, the path the caretaker walked each day. It led straight and sure across the plain toward the heart of the museum. Nia followed the drips one by one. Tock trotted at her heel. She was glad to have a track to trust, instead of guessing her way through the endless painted grass.

The trail of oil-drips ended at a plain door set in the savanna wall, half hidden behind a clump of tin reeds. Nia laid her hand against it and found the wood warm, warmer than anything else in the cold hall. Behind it something hummed, a deep and steady sound she could feel in her teeth. It was the great clock, she was sure, beating on at the center of everything. Tock scrabbled at the base of the door, eager to be through. Nia wiped her palms on her coat, took hold of the handle, and made ready to step forward.

The giraffe grove was a strange and lovely place, a forest made all of long tin necks. They rose around Nia on every side, taller than lampposts. Their spotted metal was patterned like real hide. Far up in the roof shadows she could just make out their heads. Each had two little horns and a pair of soft glass eyes. Even still and silent, they seemed kind. They bent over her like patient giants. Tock gazed up with his whiskers trembling. Nia walked slowly between the great legs and moved along, her candle lighting only the lowest reaches of the grove.

High on a shelf near one giraffe's shoulder, almost out of reach, something caught the candlelight and shone. Nia squinted up at it. A ring, she thought, a wide metal ring shaped just like a keyring, the sort a keeper might hang a whole bunch of keys upon. Beside it hung a rope on a little pulley, the very tool the keeper must have used to raise the shelf and lower it again. Her heart quickened. Keys, up there, waiting. She set down her candle, took hold of the rough rope, and got ready to bring the shelf down to her.

Nia worked the pulley hand over hand, and the shelf came creaking down toward her. But when it settled and she saw what the ring truly held, her hopes sank. No keys hung there at all, only a row of spare oil-cans, dull and heavy and useless to her. She had climbed her hopes up for nothing. Still, she did not leave empty. Painted on the wall behind the shelf was a bright arrow, pointing off through the grove, clearly marking the way onward. Nia sighed, then squared herself. An arrow was a clue too. She would follow where it led.

Following the painted arrow, Nia wound her way on through the grove, in and out among the great tin legs. The arrow led to another, and then another, a whole line of them a keeper had left to guide the way. At last they brought her to a keeper door set in the far wall. She pressed her hand flat to it, the way she had learned to, and felt the warmth and the deep steady hum of the museum's heart beating close behind. Tock chirped and scampered at her feet. The arrows had not lied. This was the road inward.

Both routes across the savanna met at the same spot in the end, a tall arch in the inner wall that opened onto the central rotunda. Pale light leaked around its edges. Nia could feel the great clock humming on the far side, closer now than ever. Quick as a flick of thought, Tock darted ahead of her, slipping under the arch with a flick of his little tin tail and disappearing into the room beyond. Nia shook her head with a fond smile. He never could wait. She stepped up to the arch and looked through into the light.

Beyond the arch the great rotunda waited for her, round and grand and quiet. Its high skylight let down a wash of soft grey light, the first pale hint of the dawn that was still to come. The wide circular floor swept away under her feet, worn smooth by countless visitors over countless years. Nia knew this place for what it was, the true middle of the whole clockwork museum, the still heart that all the halls curled around. Her footsteps whispered as she came in. Ahead of her, in the center, Tock sat waiting and ticking, proud to have led her true.

Rising in the very center of the rotunda towered the great museum clock. Its round face hung pale and huge above Nia, the long hands stopped dead at some forgotten hour. Little shelves and hooks ringed its wide base, the sort of place a careful keeper would keep the tools of the trade. Nia's heart thumped hard. Everything had pointed her here, to the heart of the heart, and she felt sure of it now. The keys that could wake the sleeping menagerie must hang somewhere close to this great clock. She hurried forward into the rotunda to hunt for them.

The horizon-wall walkway was far narrower than it had looked. It was just a thin ledge between the painted sky and a long drop to the floor. Nia edged along it sideways, back to the wall, one careful foot after another. She was partway across when the half-wound elephant below jerked awake. It swung its heavy trunk up and out with a great groan. The trunk swept right across the way ahead, blocking the path. Nia stopped short and pressed to the wall. Her heart raced. The trunk swayed there, huge and blind, and she had to choose what to do.

choices.label: Press into an alcove and wait.
Try to hurry past the elephant.

nodes.body: Nia did not try to dodge the swinging trunk. Instead she pressed herself flat into a shallow alcove in the wall, made herself small, and held perfectly still. She waited there while the great elephant swayed and groaned above the floor, its trunk cutting the air where she would have been. She did not move a muscle. Slowly, slowly, the beast lost the last of its wind and began to settle, its groans fading to silence. Only when it stood quiet again did Nia let herself breathe out and slip carefully back down to the safe open floor.

Back on the savanna floor, the elephant stood still and silent once more. Nia looked up at the narrow wall walkway and made up her mind. That ledge was too thin, and the waking giant too near. One more scare like that was one too many. She would not go back up there. Some paths were simply not worth the risk. She brushed the dust from her coat and gave Tock a steadying pat. Then she turned to pick her way across the open floor, among the big animals, where at least she could see all around her.

Nia should have waited. Instead she tried to hurry past on the narrow walkway. The lurching elephant swung round toward the sound of her. Gently and blindly, it backed her into the far corner of the hall. Now she could not get by at all. There was no harm in it, only a big slow tin beast standing squarely in her way. She was quite safe, tucked into the corner with Tock in her lap. But there was nothing to do now except wait. In the morning the keepers would wind the elephant down and set her free.

The copper deep-sea hall was cool and blue and hushed, like stepping down under the waves. Great copper whales hung overhead on thin wires, drifting ever so slightly in the still air. In one corner a huge octopus lay coiled, its long arms curled and quiet. All around stood glass tanks of copper fish, each one caught mid-swim, glinting faintly in the dim light. Nia felt as if she had walked to the bottom of the sea. Tock ticked softly by her ear, urging her on. She drew a breath and waded into the blue and silent hall.

A little way into the blue hall, Nia found two ways to go. On the low floor, glass tanks stood in long rows, and she could wade between them toward the middle of the hall, staying safe and near the ground. Or she could climb the whale-rib catwalk, a narrow bridge of curved copper ribs that arched high over everything, up in the dark near the drifting whales. The floor path looked calm. The high catwalk looked daring. Tock's whiskers quivered as he weighed them too. Nia looked from the low tanks to the climbing bridge and chose.

Down among the floor tanks the light glimmered blue and green. It shone back off a thousand still copper scales. Nia moved slowly between the glass walls. Two tanks drew her more than the rest. One was the coral cabinet, bright and busy. It was crowded with little copper seahorses and darting fish. The other was the deep trench diorama. Its water-glass turned almost black, hiding whatever lay at the bottom. Tock looked from the bright one to the dark one and back again. Nia weighed the cheerful coral against the shadowy trench, and she decided which to open first.

The coral cabinet glittered like a treasure box. Behind its glass, copper seahorses curled among branches of bright metal coral. Little fish hung frozen in the middle of a dart, all of them wound down and still. Nia leaned close and smiled at the tiny frozen world. Then she rested her hand on the cabinet's side, and she felt something odd. A small brass valve there hummed faintly under her palm. It was warm and alive when everything else was cold. Tock stood up on her shoulder, ears pricked. That little humming valve was worth a closer look, and Nia meant to give it one.

Tock leapt down and tapped the humming valve with both front paws, and Nia heard a soft click. A hidden panel in the cabinet slid smoothly back. Inside lay a folded chart, and when she drew it out and opened it in the candlelight, her eyes went wide. It was a map of the whole museum, every single hall drawn out in careful ink, the aviary and the savanna and this deep-sea hall, all joined by little corridors. Someone had made it with great care long ago. Nia smoothed the old paper flat. With a map like this, she need never be lost again.

Studying the chart by candlelight, Nia found a small tag tied to one corner with thread. It was cut in the shape of a key. Beside it, in the same neat hand that had drawn the map, a short note had been written. It pointed to the rotunda at the very middle of the museum, the round hall at the building's heart, and hinted that something important waited there. Nia's pulse quickened. A key-shaped tag, and an arrow to the heart of it all. She was beginning to understand. Whatever could wake this sleeping place, the way to it ran straight to the rotunda.

Nia folded the chart carefully and tucked it away where it would stay safe. Then she set off along its inked corridors, with Tock scampering ahead. The map made the dark hall far less frightening. Before, there had been only shadows and guesswork. Now there was a clear line to follow, corridor by corridor, toward the warm middle of the building. She kept her candle low and her steps sure. Tock checked back over his shoulder now and then, as if making certain she still followed. Together they threaded their way inward, closer to the heart with every turn.

The mapped way ended at a round copper door, set in the wall like the porthole of a ship. Nia rested her hand on it and felt it hum, warm beneath her fingers. Somewhere just beyond, close now, the great clock ticked its deep slow beat, and she could feel each tick travel through the metal into her bones. This was the heart, or near it. Tock spun an eager circle at her feet, ticking fast. Nia checked the chart one last time to be sure, then rolled it up, gripped the round handle of the porthole door, and made ready to step forward.

The deep trench diorama was the darkest place Nia had been all night. Black glass rose around her on every side. It was so dark she could barely see her own reflection. The tank seemed to fall away forever into an inky, silent deep. Only one thing broke the blackness. Far down at the bottom shone a faint and lonely glow, small as a distant star. Nia's skin prickled. She did not much like the dark. But the glow tugged at her, and Tock ticked bravely by her ear. So she swallowed hard and moved along toward it.

The faint glow, Nia saw as she drew near, came from an anglerfish, a strange deep-sea creature with a little glowing lamp hung out on a stalk before its face. This one still had a scrap of wind left in it, just enough to keep its lamp softly burning. By that small light she could see a little more of the trench now. And there, set low in the tank wall where the glow just reached, was a hatch, a small square door she would never have found in the dark. Nia crept closer to it, careful and quiet, and looked.

The anglerfish lamp flickered, dimming and brightening as its last wind ran down, and by its uneven glow Nia read a sign painted above the little hatch. It was a keeper sign, she was sure. An arrow pointed inward through the hatch, and beside it someone had painted the plain shape of a clock. She knew that mark by now. It meant the heart of the museum, the place with the great clock, the place she had been trying to reach all night. Nia's heart lifted. The dark trench was not a dead end after all; it was a doorway.

Nia took hold of the little hatch and swung it open. Beyond lay a snug copper passage, only just wide enough to crawl into. It curved away inward through the dark. Warm air breathed out of it against her face. With it came the deep steady hum she now knew so well. She ducked her head and tucked her candle carefully, so its flame would not catch her sleeve. Then she followed the passage in. Tock scurried ahead, his ticking bright in the close copper tunnel. Warm and humming, the way led her steadily toward the center of the museum.

Both ways through the deep-sea hall brought Nia to the same door in the end, the round copper door that opened onto the central rotunda. A thread of pale light glowed around its rim. Right through the metal she could feel the great clock's hum, stronger than ever now. Before she could grip the handle, Tock squeezed himself eagerly through the gap at the door's edge, wriggling his little tin body until he popped through and vanished into the room beyond. Nia laughed softly. He simply could not wait for her. She set both hands to the round door and pushed it open.

Past the copper door the great rotunda opened wide. Nia stepped out of the cramped dark into a grand round hall. High above, a broad skylight let down the soft grey light of the coming dawn. It felt cool and clean after the blue deep. The wide floor curved away beneath her, worn smooth by years of visiting feet. She knew it in an instant, the true heart of the whole clockwork museum. Her steps echoed gently round the walls. And there in the middle sat Tock, waiting for her, ticking with pride at having found the way.

At the center of the rotunda the huge museum clock stood tall, towering over Nia's upturned face. Its great round face was pale in the dawn light, its hands stopped and still. A ring of little hooks and shelves circled its base, just the place a keeper might keep small and precious things. Nia's heart drummed. All night the museum had seemed to point her here, and now she was certain. The keys that could wake the whole sleeping menagerie must hang somewhere close beside this clock. She could almost feel them near. She hurried forward across the rotunda to search.

The whale-rib catwalk swayed the moment Nia set her weight on it, creaking and shifting as she climbed higher into the dark. It was made of curved copper ribs joined end to end, arching out over the very middle of the hall, far above the tanks below. She was partway across when one loose rib swung away from its neighbor with a sudden clang, leaving a black gap in the bridge, a gap she would have to jump to go on. Nia clung to the guide rope, breathing hard. She could not see the far side well at all. She had to decide.

choices.label: Cling to the rope and back off.
Try to jump the gap in the dark.

nodes.body: Nia did not jump. She could not see the far side of the gap, and a jump in the dark was no jump at all, only a guess. So she backed away from the swinging rib instead, both hands locked tight on the guide rope, and worked her way carefully backward along the catwalk. Rib by rib she went, testing each one before she trusted her weight to it. Her hands ached from gripping so hard, and her arms shook. But bit by bit she reached the solid start of the bridge again, safe, with Tock still clinging to her collar.

Nia stood safe at the foot of the catwalk once more. She looked back up at the high dark crossing and shook her head firmly. A leap she could not see the end of was no way to cross anything. It simply was not worth it, not for all the wonders in the world. She rubbed her aching hands warm again and steadied her breathing. Then she turned away from the swaying bridge for good. Instead she waded in among the floor tanks below. There the ground was solid, and she could see each careful step by the glow of the glass.

Nia should have backed off, but she tried to jump the gap in the dark. Halfway into the leap she saw she could not tell where the far side was, and fear froze her stiff on the swinging rib. She could not go on, and now she dared not go back. She clung there, safe above the soft nets strung over the tanks below, and made herself hold still and breathe. She was not falling. She was only stuck. Down below, Tock squeaked and squeaked, and at opening time a keeper heard him and came to help her gently down, her long night of exploring over.

ending.title: Lost in the Dark Deep Hall

nodes.body: The crystal insect conservatory glowed softly, and Nia caught her breath. All around, clockwork butterflies and beetles rested on ferns made of pale green glass. Their wings and shells caught the light like jewels. Above her arched a great glass dome. Its inside was painted with silver stars, so she seemed to stand under a whole tiny night sky. Everything was still. Everything glittered. It was the loveliest room she had found. Tock ticked in wonder on her shoulder. Nia smiled and wandered in among the glass ferns, careful not to brush a single one.

choices.label: Wander in among the glass ferns.

nodes.body: Deeper in the conservatory, Nia found two ways she might explore. Down on the floor the low fern beds spread out before her, thick with resting insects she could walk among and study close. Up above, an iron mezzanine gallery ran all the way around the glass walls, a high balcony looking down over everything. From up there she might see the whole starry room at once. The fern beds felt safe and near. The high gallery felt grand but far. Tock turned this way and that, unsure. Nia looked from the low beds to the climbing gallery and chose her way.

Down in the fern beds the glass leaves chimed faintly when Nia's coat brushed them, a sound like tiny bells. She trod softly between them. Two places drew her most. On one side lay the butterfly vault, a quiet nook where clockwork butterflies hung with their foil wings folded and still. On the other side sat the beetle corner, where dozens of little brass beetles crowded around an old keeper bench cluttered with tools. Tock's nose twitched toward both at once. Nia weighed the sleeping butterflies against the busy little beetles and made up her mind which to visit.

The butterfly vault was a hushed and glittering place. Clockwork butterflies hung everywhere Nia looked, their wings made of thin bright foil in every color, folded neatly shut and perfectly still. They were waiting, she thought, waiting for a wind that had never come to lift them. She held her candle close, and the little folded wings threw back sparks of red and gold and blue. She hardly dared breathe near them, they looked so delicate. Tock gazed up, his whiskers trembling. Nia stood a moment in the quiet beauty of it all, then made herself go on and look further.

Tock hopped down and nosed at a tiny winder left lying on a stand. It was a little key, smaller than a matchstick. Nia picked it up and looked closer at the butterflies. Now she understood what it was for. Each butterfly had a keyhole in its body. But such a keyhole, no bigger than a poppy seed! No ordinary key could ever fit it, only this tiny winder made just for them. She turned the little tool over in her fingers. Whoever built these creatures had loved even their smallest parts. She pressed on deeper.

Beside the winder's stand a small card was propped, and Nia bent to read it. It told of one great key, a master key. That key could turn the whole museum at once. It woke every creature together from the central rotunda, the hall at the building's heart. She read the card twice to be sure. So there was a single key that ruled them all, and it waited in the middle of everything. Nia fixed the way to the rotunda firmly in her mind. Then she tucked the little winder safely into her pocket and got ready to follow it there.

Nia followed the painted path that wound out of the butterfly vault. A keeper's careful hand had marked it here and there on the floor. Tock scurried ahead of her, weaving in and out between the glass ferns. His ticking was bright in the quiet. She went slowly and minded her elbows, so as not to knock a single delicate leaf. The path curved gently onward, always bending toward the middle of the museum. Around her the painted stars glittered on the dome. With Tock to lead and the path to follow, Nia made her steady way deeper toward the heart.

The painted path ended at a mirrored door, edged all around with polished brass. Nia saw herself reflected in it, small and tired and brave. Her candle was a wavering point of gold. She laid her hand on the glass, and it was warm. Behind it she felt the great clock beating, slow and deep, a heartbeat she could feel in her palm. Tock stood up tall beside her, quivering. This was the way to the middle. She was sure of it. Nia straightened her shoulders, wrapped her fingers around the brass handle, and made ready to step forward.

The beetle corner was a busy little world all its own. Dozens of small brass beetles crowded together around an old keeper bench. It was as if they had gathered for a meeting and then all wound down at once. The bench was scattered with tiny tools. There were pin-sized screwdrivers, little oil-pots, and coils of thin wire, all needed to tend such tiny creatures. Nia leaned in, charmed. It felt like peeking into a workshop for fairies. Tock sniffed at a beetle, whiskers twitching. She let her eyes wander over the cluttered bench, then moved along to see more.

In a half-open drawer of the keeper bench, Nia found something better than any tool. Folded small and soft with age lay a map, and when she spread it out she saw it showed the conservatory and the halls that lay beyond it too. It was drawn in a keeper's careful hand, every path and door marked plainly. Her heart lifted. No more guessing which way to turn in the dark. She had a map now, a real one, made by someone who knew this place inside and out. Tock peered at it beside her. Nia smoothed the creases flat and studied where the marked ways led.

Tracing the map by candlelight, Nia found a short clear way drawn straight to the rotunda, the round hall at the very center of the museum. And there, right along that route, someone had inked a tiny beetle, its little feelers stretched out as if pointing the way onward. She smiled at the small joke of it. The keeper who made this map had a kind sense of fun. She traced the beetle's path with her fingertip, fixing it in her memory. This was the road to the heart, marked and clear. Nia folded the map and pressed on deeper.

Nia followed the mapped way between the glass cases. She stepped carefully, so her coat would not chime against the fragile leaves. The route wound on just as the drawing promised. It led past resting beetles and folded butterflies, always bending inward. At last it brought her to a mirrored door edged with brass. She laid her hand on it and felt it warm beneath her palm. She felt too the deep hum of the museum's heart, beating close behind. Tock ticked with excitement at her feet. The map had led her true. This door opened toward the very middle of everything.

Both ways through the conservatory brought Nia to the same place, the tall arch that opened onto the central rotunda. Pale grey light spilled softly around its edges. She could feel the great clock humming now, closer than it had ever been, right through the soles of her shoes. Before she could reach the arch, Tock slipped away from her shoulder and squeezed through the narrow crack ahead of her, vanishing into the light beyond with a happy squeak. Nia smiled and shook her head. The little mouse always went first. She stepped up to the arch and looked through into the room ahead.

The arch gave onto the great rotunda, and Nia stepped through into a wide round hall. High overhead a broad skylight let down the soft grey light of the new dawn. It was cool and pale and lovely. The floor curved away beneath her, worn smooth by years of visiting feet. This was it. She knew it for certain, the true center of the whole clockwork museum. Her footsteps whispered as she came in. And there in the middle waited Tock. He sat up tall and ticked proudly, as if to say well done for coming so far.

There in the center of the rotunda the great museum clock rose up above her, tall and grand, its wide face pale and its hands frozen still. Around its base ran a ring of little hooks and shelves, just the sort of place a keeper would hang the tools of the trade. Nia's heart began to pound. Everything, all night long, had pointed her toward this one spot, the heart of the heart. She knew it now beyond any doubt. The keys that could wake the whole menagerie must hang somewhere close by the clock. She hurried forward across the rotunda to look for them.

The mezzanine gallery ran high around the glass walls, its edge lined with tall old panes of glass set in iron frames. Nia climbed to it and walked along, and it was grand up there, the whole starry room spread out below. She leaned out to look down. At once one tall panel shifted loose beside her with a low grinding sound and began to tip slowly inward, leaning toward her over the long drop. Nia went very still. The loose glass creaked. She was too close to it, and it was too big and too heavy. She had to decide, right now, what to do.

choices.label: Step back from the loose glass.

nodes.body: Nia snatched her hands back from the rail and stepped smartly away from the loose glass. She gave it all the room it wanted. The tall panel creaked, and tipped, and then settled crookedly against the rail. It leaned there, but it no longer fell, safe so long as she left it alone. She let out a shaky breath. That had been far too close. She did not try to hold it or push it back. She only backed away along the gallery, slow and careful, well clear of the leaning pane, until she reached the safe stairs down.

Back down on the conservatory floor, Nia was still a little shaken. She looked up at the mezzanine gallery and shook her head. All that old glass sat loose in its frames, ready to tip at a touch. It was simply too fragile to trust. One more shift like that might have ended badly. Some high places were best left to the keepers. She steadied herself with a slow breath and a pat for Tock. Then she turned away from the gallery for good. Instead she went to explore the fern beds below, where the danger was far less.

Nia should have stepped back. Instead she edged past the loose panel. As she squeezed by, another pane shifted behind her. With a soft sigh a glass door swung shut across the gallery. It closed her gently into a bright little dead end. Nothing broke. Nothing fell. She was quite safe, boxed in by clear glass, the starry dome shining above her. But she could not get out. There was nothing to do now but tap on the glass to Tock on the far side. So she waited, patient and calm, until the keepers came in the morning to open the gallery.

Every wing of the museum had led Nia here, to the great central rotunda. And here at last she found what she had come for. High on a brass hook beside the towering clock hung a single key. It was bigger and grander than any other, worn bright with years of use. The caretaker's master key, she knew at once, the one key that could wind the whole menagerie. A little keeper ladder stood against the clock. Nia climbed it carefully, rung by rung, and lifted the key down. It was warm and heavy in her hands, and Tock cheered in tiny squeaks. Three ways led on now: the tall spring-room doors, the gallery of keeper portraits, and the narrow back stairs into the dark.

choices.label: Push through the great spring-room doors.
Follow the gallery of keeper portraits.
Slip down the narrow back stairs.

nodes.body: The tall spring-room doors swung open, and Nia stepped into a vast machine room. She stopped and stared upward. This was where the whole museum's clockwork lived, a forest of wheels and shafts and gears reaching up and up into the dark far above her head. Everything was still. Nothing turned. It was like standing inside a giant, stopped clock. The master key hung heavy in her hand, and Tock pressed close against her neck. Somewhere in all this silent machinery, she felt sure, was the reason the animals slept. She lifted her candle and went deeper, toward the heart of the works.

Nia followed a narrow gantry that ran between the still, towering gears, a metal walkway just wide enough for her feet. Tock pattered ahead, his little claws ticking on the iron. On either side rose wheels taller than she was, their teeth locked together, frozen mid-turn. She kept one hand on the rail and did not look down. Ahead, in the very middle of the great machine, loomed a huge silent drum, bigger than anything else in the room. It drew her eye and would not let go. Whatever slept at the center of all this, it was there. She went on toward it.

The gantry ended above the biggest mechanism Nia had ever seen. She gripped the rail and gazed down at it, awed into silence. This was the master works of the entire menagerie, the great engine that drove every bird and beast in the museum, and it lay cold and quiet and still. No wheel turned. No shaft moved. All that mighty machinery waited, hushed, as if holding its breath. Nia felt very small above it. Tock crept onto her shoulder and went quiet too. Somewhere down there was the secret of the long sleep, and she leaned out to look closer.

The gallery of keeper portraits stretched away before Nia, long and narrow. Paintings lined both walls. Old caretakers looked down at her from their frames. They were men and women in oil-stained aprons, each holding some little clockwork creature with pride. In the dim candlelight their painted eyes seemed to follow her. It was not unkindly. It was as if they were curious about the child walking their hall at night. Far at the end stood a single closed door. Nia held the master key tight. She walked on down the row of watching faces, toward the door at the far end.

The last portrait in the row made Nia stop and look. It showed a kind old man with a gentle, tired smile and hands stained dark with oil, a man who plainly loved his work. Beneath the frame a small brass plate had been fixed. She held her candle to it and read the words engraved there: Keeper Fettle, tender of the works. So this was the caretaker of the whole museum, the one who kept every creature wound and well. Nia looked up at his painted face and felt she liked him already. Then she turned to the door beneath his portrait.

The door below Keeper Fettle's portrait opened, and Nia found herself on a high walkway looking down into a vast room. Below her spread the great machine room, the whole clockwork heart of the museum, wheels and shafts and gears all locked and silent in the dark. It was where old Fettle must have spent his days, she thought, tending the mighty works. The master key hung warm in her hand. Tock leaned out beside her to peer down at the huge quiet engine. This was surely the center of everything. Nia followed the walkway on, down toward the silent heart of the museum.

The narrow back stairs were cramped and dark, spiraling down so tightly that Nia had to turn her shoulders to fit. Her candle threw jumping shadows on the close stone walls. At the very bottom a keeper turnstile barred the way, an old iron gate meant to let one person through at a time, its lock stiff and rusted with age. It would not simply open. Nia studied it. There was a narrow gap along one side she might just squeeze through if she went carefully, or she could try shoving hard against the turnstile itself. She weighed the two ways in the flickering light.

Nia chose the careful way. She breathed in deep, made herself as thin as she could, and slipped sideways into the narrow gap beside the turnstile. The cold iron pressed against her back and her front, but she did not force it; she eased through inch by inch, patient and slow. Tock rode along on her collar, tucked in tight. For a moment she was snugly stuck, and then she was through, popping out into a low passage on the far side. She let out her breath with a grin. Gentle and slow had done it. The way ahead lay open before her.

The low passage climbed gently upward, and Nia followed it with Tock trotting ahead. After a little way it opened out, and she found herself on a ledge high above a vast room. She caught her breath. Below her spread the great machine room, the master works of the whole museum, a huge tangle of wheels and shafts and gears lying cold and silent in the dark. So this was where the back stairs led, straight to the heart of everything. The master key hung heavy in her hand. Nia found the way down and began to climb toward the silent works below.

Nia should have gone gently, but she shoved the old turnstile with all her weight instead. It swung the wrong way with a screech, and before she could stop it, it latched shut fast behind her, penning her in the little dark stairwell with no way back up. She pushed and pulled, but the rusted gate would not budge. Nia sat down on the cold steps to think. She was safe, and Tock nestled warm in her lap to keep her company. But she was well and truly stuck now, and all she could do was wait for the morning staff to come and unlatch the turnstile and let her out.

All three ways brought Nia out on a high ledge above the great spring-room. And here at last she learned why the whole menagerie had fallen asleep. Below her lay the enormous mainspring that drove every single animal. It had snapped clean across. Its broken ends had sprung apart, curled back like a broken watch. Nothing could be wound until it was mended. Even the master key in her hand was useless against a broken heart of steel. Tock scratched worriedly at the floor. Nia looked about for help and saw three things: a keeper toolbox, a tall wall of repair manuals, and a scratched patch of floor where Tock kept digging.

Nia went first to the keeper toolbox. It was a deep tin chest, dented and old, and when she lifted the lid it gave a rusty squeak. Inside lay a jumble of spanners and oil-cans and rags, all smelling of metal and grease. On the very top rested one heavy tool she had never seen before, oddly shaped, with a great clamp at one end. She turned it over in her hands, wondering what on earth it was for. It felt important, made for one special job. Tock sniffed at it with interest. Nia set it carefully aside and looked for something that might tell her more.

Tock burrowed down under the loose tools, and after a moment he turned up a stiff card and dragged it into the light. Nia took it and held her candle close. It was a keeper's instruction card, a careful drawing that showed, step by step, how a broken mainspring was clamped tight and then changed for a new one. And there in the drawing was the very tool she had just been holding, the heavy clamp, doing its special job. Her heart lifted. Now the strange tool made sense. She was beginning to understand how the great spring might be mended after all.

Nia held the instruction card in one hand. She tucked the heavy tool under her arm. Then she looked for where to go next. On the wall beside the toolbox a keeper sign was painted. It was a small arrow, pointing off past the great machine. She followed where it led. She picked her way carefully among the silent gears, Tock trotting close at her heel. The arrow brought her toward a low door, half hidden behind the huge dark mechanism. You would never notice it unless you were looking. Nia went toward it, sure it led somewhere that mattered.

Nia crossed to the tall wall of manuals. It was stacked from floor to ceiling with worn keeper books, their leather spines cracked and their gold letters faded soft. She tilted her head to read them. One said Winding. Another said Oiling. And there, near the middle, one thick spine read Mending the Great Spring. Her breath caught. That was exactly what she needed. Someone had written down how to do this very thing, and here it waited on the shelf. Tock ran up and down the row as if reading the titles too. Nia reached for the mending book and eased it out.

Nia pulled down the heavy mending book and let it fall open in her hands. Its pages were soft with age and dark with old fingerprints, and they fell open all on their own to a much-used place near the middle. There, drawn in careful ink, was a picture of a hidden workshop, a snug little room where every spare part a keeper might need was kept in neat rows. Nia's eyes widened. So there was such a place, a storeroom of springs and tools, somewhere close by. If she could only find it, she might have everything she needed. She read on eagerly.

The book told Nia just where to look. In plain words it named a door, a hidden one, set behind the great machine itself. It even drew a little map of how to reach it. She snapped the book shut and hurried off at once. Tock scampered along at her heels. Around the huge dark mechanism she went, running her hand along the cold metal. She searched for the door the book had promised. Her candle bobbed, and her heart raced. Somewhere here, tucked out of sight, was the way to the workshop, and everything a keeper kept inside it.

Where Tock kept scratching, Nia knelt and looked. A floor panel there sat loose in its frame, and when she pried at its edge she felt a cool draft beneath, a hidden crawlspace running under the floor. But there was a danger. A heavy gear leaned right over the panel, only half-braced, held up by nothing but an old wooden wedge. If it came down, it would come down hard. Nia looked from the panel to the leaning gear and knew she must be careful. She could ease the panel up slowly and gently, or yank it free all at once. She thought hard about which.

Nia chose to go slow. She lifted the loose panel a careful inch at a time, watching the leaning gear all the while, ready to freeze if it so much as trembled. She kept her hands well clear of where it might fall. The panel rose gently, gently, without a single sudden jerk, until it came away clean in her hands and she set it aside. Below lay a low crawlspace running off into the dark behind the great machine. The leaning gear had not shifted at all. Nia let out a breath and smiled. Careful had kept her safe, and now the hidden way lay open.

Nia got down on her hands and knees and crawled into the low space, Tock scampering just ahead to light the way with his bright little ticking. The crawlspace was snug and dusty, running along behind the great silent machine. She kept her head down and pushed on, and before long the passage opened up and let her stand. There, right in front of her, was a small hidden door, plain and low, the very one Keeper Fettle must have used a thousand times. Her heart leapt. She had found the secret way in. Nia reached out to open the hidden door.

Nia should have gone gently, but she yanked the panel free all at once instead. The sudden jerk was too much for the half-braced gear. With a great clang it toppled over and crashed down across the crawlspace, blocking it completely, and worse, it pinned the hem of her coat fast beneath its edge. Nia's heart jumped, but she kept her head. She wriggled carefully out of her coat sleeve by sleeve, and slipped free, unhurt. But the way below was buried now, quite out of reach. There was nothing to do but wait by the broken spring until the keepers came at dawn to shift the fallen gear.

Each way had led to the same low door, and when Nia opened it she found the caretaker's hidden workshop, snug and warm and full of wonders. Spare springs hung coiled on pegs along the walls. There were oil-cans and clamps and, on a hook of its own, the special tool made just for changing a mainspring. On the bench lay a letter in Keeper Fettle's own hand. Nia read it by candlelight. He had been taken poorly, it said, and could not come, and he had left careful drawings of how to mend the great spring for whoever might find them. Now Nia had the master key, a spare mainspring within reach, and the knowledge to use it. Three things called to her: the open manual, the coiled spring, and Fettle's letter.

Nia turned first to the repair manual. It lay open on the bench, at the very page she needed. She bent over it and traced the lines with her finger. Each step of changing the great spring was drawn there in patient keeper ink. The clamping, the easing, the fitting, all clear and plain, one picture after another. Whoever made this had meant it for careful hands, even hands as small as hers. Tock climbed onto the bench and peered at the drawings too. Nia read slowly and made sure she understood, before she would trust herself to try it.

Nia read the manual through twice, from the first step to the last, until she was quite sure she understood every part of it. She would not rush this. Tock trotted along the page beside her finger, stopping where she stopped, for all the world like a tiny student following the lesson. The drawings made it plain: clamp the broken ends, ease them apart, fit the fresh spring, and only then wind. She said the steps softly to herself to fix them in her mind. When at last she looked up, she felt ready. The task no longer seemed quite so frightening.

Confident now, and sure of every step, Nia gathered up what she would need. She took the special changing tool from its hook, heavy and cold in her hands, and lifted a coiled spare spring down from its peg. Then she turned and headed back the way she had come, toward the great spring-room where the broken mainspring waited. Tock rode on her shoulder, keeping watch. She walked carefully, for her arms were full and the tool was awkward, but her heart was steady. She knew what to do. All that was left now was to go and begin the mending.

Nia went straight to the spare mainspring where it hung coiled and gleaming on its peg. It was a splendid thing, as long as she was tall when she stretched it out, its metal bright and new. She reached up and lifted it down, and gasped. It was far heavier than it looked, a great cold weight that dragged at her arms. She could hardly hold it. This was no thing to carry lightly across the whole museum. Tock chirped a warning from her shoulder. Nia set the spring down for a moment and looked about the workshop for some clever way to move so heavy a load.

Nia found the answer in a corner of the workshop. It was a little keeper cart, low and sturdy, made just for hauling heavy things. She wheeled it over. Then, with a good deal of huffing and careful lifting, she loaded the great spare spring onto it. Beside the spring she laid the special changing tool. Now she would have both together when she reached the spring-room. Tock hopped up and perched proudly on top to keep watch. Nia wiped her brow and took hold of the cart's handle. At least now she need not carry all that weight in her own two arms.

Nia wheeled the loaded cart carefully back the way she had come. It rolled out of the snug workshop and toward the broken heart of the museum. She went slowly, for the spring was heavy and the floor was uneven. She did not want to tip her precious load. At every bump she slowed and steadied it with one hand. Tock rode on top, ticking cheerfully, keeping his balance like a little sailor at sea. Step by careful step the cart rolled on toward the great spring-room. There the snapped mainspring waited, and the real work would begin at last.

Nia read Keeper Fettle's letter last, and near the bottom she found a curious note. It spoke of an old shortcut lever, stiff and rusted with age. That lever could send a loaded cart speeding straight back to the spring-room, saving all the heavy carrying. She looked at the rusted lever across the workshop, and then at the great heavy spring she must move. She could throw the lever and let the cart rush the spring back in a hurry. Or she could carry the spring herself, the long slow safe way, in her own two tired arms. Nia thought it over and weighed her difficult choice.

choices.label: Carry the spring the long safe way.

nodes.body: Nia chose the safe way. She would not trust a rusted old lever with something so precious. Instead she bent, wrapped both arms around the heavy spare spring, and lifted it against her chest. It was a great cold weight, and her arms shook, but she held it fast. Step by careful step she carried it out of the workshop and back toward the great spring-room, resting when she had to, never rushing. Tock trotted alongside, cheering her on with bright little squeaks. It was slow going, and hard, but the spring was safe in her own two arms, and that was what mattered.

At last, breathing hard, Nia reached the great spring-room and set the heavy spare spring down beside the broken one with a careful thump. Her arms ached and her forehead was damp, but she had done it, and nothing had come to harm. She straightened up and looked around her. Here was the snapped mainspring, and here the shining new one, and she had the master key and the tool and the knowledge too. Everything she needed was gathered now in one place. Tock hopped down and turned in a happy circle. Nia caught her breath and turned to face the great broken spring.

Nia should have carried it herself, but she threw the rusted shortcut lever instead. With a groan and a jerk the old cart lurched away far too fast, rattling off down a side track with the precious spare spring aboard. Nia ran after it, but it was too quick. It rolled straight through a gap and vanished behind a heavy locked shutter that clanged down after it. Nothing was broken, and Nia was quite unhurt. But the spare spring was out of reach now, shut away where she could not follow. All she could do was wait by the silent machine until the keepers came in the morning to open the shutter.

Every path had ended here, at the great spring-room, and now Nia stood ready. In one hand she held the master key. At her feet lay the shining spare mainspring. And clear in her head were Keeper Fettle's careful instructions, learned by heart. Through the tall windows the sky was turning the soft grey of dawn. Soon the doors would open and children would pour in, hoping to see the animals move. There was time for one brave choice. Nia could try to change the great spring and rewind the whole menagerie, or mend just one beloved creature she had come to love, or follow Keeper Fettle's deepest secret, or simply keep the museum safe and let the quiet dawn come.

choices.label: Change the great spring and rewind them all.
Keep the museum safe until dawn.

nodes.body: Changing the great mainspring was the hardest task of all, and Nia knew that how she did it would matter. This was the whole heart of the museum; there was no room for a careless slip. She looked at the broken spring, and the shining new one, and Fettle's drawings spread beside her. She could work slow and careful, following the manual step by patient step. She could work bold and fast, racing to finish before the dawn came. Or she could try the clever little trick she had spotted in Fettle's notes, meant to make the whole job easier. Nia drew a breath and thought about which way to begin.

choices.label: Work slow and careful by the manual.
Work bold and fast to beat the dawn.

nodes.body: Nia chose the slow and careful way, and she took her time with every step. First she clamped the broken spring tight, just as the manual showed, so it could not spring loose and hurt her. Then, little by little, she eased the snapped ends apart. At last she fitted the shining spare into its place, checking the drawing after each move to be sure she had it right. Tock made himself useful, passing her each tool as she needed it, tiny paws steady. It was slow work, but it was good work, and Nia felt it going right beneath her careful hands.

choices.label: Fit the master key to the shaft.

nodes.body: The new spring was seated snug and true. The master key slid into the great winding shaft as if it had always belonged there. Nia rested her hand on it, ready. Now came the choice. She could wind the key fully, right now. Then the whole menagerie would leap awake for the morning crowd, every bird and beast at once. Or she could wind it gently, only part way. Then the works would stir softly when Keeper Fettle came home and turned the key himself. Nia thought of the waiting children, and of the kind old keeper. Then she made her choice.

choices.label: Wind it fully and wake them all for dawn.
Set them to wake when Fettle returns.

nodes.body: Nia chose to work bold and fast, racing the growing light in the windows. Her hands flew, clamping and easing and fitting, quicker than the manual meant her to go. She forced the shining spring down into its place. It was nearly seated, nearly right, but not quite; one end still stood a hair too proud of the shaft. She could feel it was not perfect. Now she must choose, and choose fast. She could stop and take the time to reseat it properly, or she could wind it as it was, right now, and simply hope that it would hold.

Nia made herself stop. She would not wind a spring she knew was not seated right, not with the whole museum resting on it. So she slowed her racing hands, backed the spring carefully out again, and fitted it once more, this time nice and true. It clicked home properly. When she set the master key to the great works and turned, the mechanism took it with a deep and satisfying click that she felt right through her arms. That was the sound of a job done right. Nia grinned, breathless. Now she could wind the museum awake and trust it to hold.

Nia chose to try Keeper Fettle's clever trick. In his notes he had drawn a small helper gear, and shown how, if she linked the spare spring through it just so, the gear would take half the strain of seating the heavy spring. She studied the drawing, then set it up with care, fitting the little gear into place and threading the spring through as Fettle showed. And it worked. The great spring slid home far smoother than she had dared to hope, the helper gear sharing the load. Nia laughed under her breath. The old keeper had thought of everything. The hardest part was suddenly easy.

The clever trick was done, and the helper gear shared the strain. The great mainspring held firm and true in its place. Nia fitted the master key to the winding shaft and turned it. The key moved smoothly and easily. Deep in the machine a low waking hum began to build. It spread outward through wheel and shaft and wire. She could feel the whole museum stirring around her, warming, quickening, coming back to life at her hand. Tock spun in a delighted circle. Nia held her breath and watched. All through the halls, the sleeping menagerie began at last to stir.

Nia wound the master key, and all through the museum the menagerie woke at once. Brass birds burst into song. The tin lion stretched and shook its mane. The copper whales turned slowly on their wires. The great elephant lifted its trunk in a gentle, happy trumpet. When the doors opened at dawn, the children gasped with joy. Every animal was moving, the whole place alive with wonder. And Keeper Fettle wept with gladness when he heard the news. A kind stranger had saved his whole life's work. Of all the ways the long night might have ended, this was the very best, and Nia would remember it always.

Nia set the great spring gently, winding it only part way. Now the animals would wake slow and easy, the moment Keeper Fettle turned the key himself. She left everything mended and ready. The tool was laid by. The master key sat in its shaft, waiting for his hand. Then she wrote him a small note and slipped quietly away. When Fettle came home at last, tired and worried, he found his menagerie whole and waiting. It was ready to wake at his touch. He knew at once that a kind hand had been there in the night. It was a warm and thoughtful ending, and it made the old keeper smile.

The reseated spring held firm, and Nia wound the museum awake. All around her the menagerie stirred. Birds ruffled their wings. The lion lifted its head. The whales turned slow. Most of the animals came beautifully to life. A few small ones, far off in distant halls, slept on, for the mend was not quite perfect. But when the doors opened, the morning crowd still marveled at a whole museum of moving wonders. They barely noticed the handful that stayed still. Nia grinned, proud to have very nearly done it all. It was a fine morning's work for one brave girl and her mouse.

Nia wound the half-seated spring, hoping it would hold. But it did not. With a sharp twang the spring slipped and sprang loose from the shaft. She knew at once that the mend would have to be done all over again. Nothing was harmed, and nobody was hurt. But there was no time left now before the doors opened. And so the animals slept on through the whole morning. Nia sat down beside the quiet works, a little sad. She waited for the keepers to arrive. She would explain everything she had tried, and everything she had learned in the long night.

Nia's clever mend held strong. The little helper gear did its work, just as Fettle's notes had promised. The master key woke the menagerie smoothly. Animal after animal came alive down the halls, birds and beasts and fish all stirring in turn. That morning the keepers arrived and saw what she had done. They shook their heads in wonder at the neat helper gear tucked into the works. A child had thought of that, they said, and mended the great spring alone in the dark. Why, she has the makings of a fine keeper! Nia glowed with quiet pride at the words.

Nia knew she could not mend the whole great spring in time; that was more than one night allowed. But with the master key and a little spare wind, she could wake just one animal, one single creature brought back to life to greet the dawn. She thought of all the wonders she had met that night, and her heart tugged this way and that. There was the brass lark in the aviary, and the tin lion out on the savanna. There was the copper whale in the blue hall, and the crystal butterfly in the dome. And there was the gentle half-wound elephant. Which one should she choose?

Nia hurried all the way back to the brass aviary, the master key warm in her hand and Tock riding her shoulder. The little brass lark waited on its perch just as she had left it, beak open on a song that never came. She drew the tiny winder from her pocket, the seed-small key made for the songbirds, and fitted it with the greatest care into the lark's little keyhole. Her fingers were steady. She turned it slowly, gently, careful not to wind too hard and hurt the delicate spring inside. Bit by bit she felt it tighten. The little bird was almost ready to wake.

As the last turn wound home, the brass lark began to tremble on its perch. Its little metal feathers ruffled and settled. Its bright head tilted, first one way and then the other, as if it were waking from a long, long sleep and remembering where it was. Nia held her breath and did not dare to move. Tock watched beside her, whiskers quivering with delight, both little paws pressed together. The lark drew itself up tall, puffed out its brass chest, and opened its beak wide. Nia stepped softly back to give it room, and waited for what would come.

The brass lark burst into the sweetest clockwork song. A stream of silver notes filled the whole aviary and set the silent birds gleaming. Nia laughed for pure joy to hear it. When the doors opened at dawn, the children crowded close and hushed. They listened to the one small bird singing its heart out in the stillness. And Keeper Fettle, told of it later, smiled his tired kind smile. A museum with even one waking animal, he said, is a museum full of hope. Nia carried that thought home with her, bright as the little lark's song.

Nia crossed back to the tin savanna, out across the wide plain of painted grass, and came at last to the great lion where it stood caught mid-roar. Up close it was huge, its tin mane bristling, its jaws frozen open. She was not afraid of it now. She found the keyhole in its broad shoulder and fitted the master key, then began to wind. Round and round she turned it, feeling the spring inside grow slowly tight and ready. Tock watched from a safe perch nearby. Nia wound with care, not too fast and not too far, until the great cat was set to wake.

As the last of the wind took hold, the tin lion shivered all over, its metal mane shaking with a soft rattling sound. Slowly, slowly, it lifted its heavy head, the great jaws closing and opening again, life creeping back into the mighty beast. Nia felt her heart pound, half in wonder and half in awe. She was glad she stood a little way back. Tock had tucked himself well out of the way too. The lion drew a long breath into its tin chest, filling out, gathering itself. Nia watched, wide-eyed, as the great creature woke fully at last.

The tin lion opened its great jaws and gave a long, rolling roar. It echoed through every hall of the museum, and the sleeping building seemed to shiver at the sound. Nia laughed aloud, proud right down to her toes. When the doors opened, the morning children jumped at that mighty roar. Then they cheered and cheered, thrilled to their bones. The tin lion tossed its shining mane and roared again for them, king of the whole savanna. Nia grinned up at her brave waking beast, glad she had chosen the lion. It was a grand and thunderous end to her long night.

Nia made her way back to the copper deep-sea hall, cool and blue and quiet, where the great whales hung on their wires overhead. She picked the biggest of them all, a vast copper whale drifting high above, and looked for a way to wake it. There was a winding-place low on its side, just within reach if she stood on her toes. She stretched up with the master key and fitted it in, then began to wind, slow and steady, feeling the great spring within the whale draw tight. Tock watched from a tank below. Nia wound with care and got the giant ready to wake.

As the spring wound tight, the great whale's flukes began to stir, sweeping slowly through the still air. Its huge copper body swung gently on its wires, nose lifting, tail sinking, as if it had begun to swim through some deep unseen sea. And from somewhere inside it a sound began to build, a low deep hum that grew and grew, like a song called up from far under the waves. Nia felt it in her chest more than she heard it. She stepped softly back, gazing up, and Tock went still beside her. The whale was waking, vast and slow and beautiful.

The copper whale let out a long, low, beautiful call. It rolled all through the blue hall like the sound of the deep sea itself. The whale turned gently on its wires, as if swimming in some far ocean. Nia stood beneath it, gazing up, her heart full. When the children came at dawn, they gathered under the great whale and fell silent. They stared up with wide and wondering eyes as it sang and swam above them. Nia felt she had given the museum back its very heart. It was a deep and wondrous ending, and it stayed with her long after.

Nia slipped back into the crystal conservatory, under its dome of painted stars. The clockwork butterflies hung folded and still on their glass ferns. She picked one, a lovely thing with wings of thin bright foil. Then she took out the tiny seed-small winder made just for them. With the very lightest touch, holding her breath, she fitted it into the butterfly's poppy-seed keyhole. This was the most delicate winding of all. She turned the little key by the smallest amounts, so gently, careful not to snap the tiny spring. Tock watched, still as a statue. The butterfly trembled, ready to wake.

The foil wings gave a shiver. Then slowly they began to unfold, spreading wide for the first time in who knew how long. As they opened, they caught the grey dawn light through the dome. They broke it into a hundred tiny colors, red and gold and green and blue, scattered across the glass ferns. Nia gasped softly at the beauty of it. The little spring had taken hold. The butterfly quivered on its perch, wings trembling, ready for flight. She drew back a step, hardly daring to breathe. Tock gazed up in silent wonder beside her.

The crystal butterfly lifted from its glass fern. It began to flutter in slow, bright circles beneath the starry dome, scattering color as it flew. Nia laughed with delight. One by one she wound a few more. Soon a little cloud of drifting color floated softly through the conservatory. When the children came at dawn, they would chase the bright butterflies with shining eyes. They would reach up on tiptoe to follow them round the room. It was a delicate, magical ending, gentle as the flutter of foil wings. Nia thought it just about the loveliest sight she had ever made.

Nia went back to the tin savanna, to the great half-wound elephant that had frightened her so badly in the dark with its lurching and its groaning. She was not afraid of it now. She understood it now. It had never meant her any harm; it had only been half-wound and blind and clumsy. She found the winding-place on its broad side and fitted the master key, and this time she would wind it true and full, the way it was meant to be. Tock watched from a safe spot. Nia set her hands to the key and began, steady and sure, to make the giant whole.

As the proper wind filled its spring, the great tin elephant steadied under Nia's hands. The lurching stopped. The groaning faded away. Where before it had swayed and scraped and frightened her, now its huge feet settled square and sure, and its movements grew smooth and slow and calm. It was as if the poor half-wound beast had only needed to be made whole to become gentle. Nia felt a warm rush of gladness. Tock crept out from his hiding place, no longer afraid either. The elephant lowered its great head toward her, calm as could be, and Nia smiled up at it.

The elephant had lurched so scarily in the dark. Now it swayed gently and lifted its trunk in a soft, friendly trumpet. It was tame and calm, no longer a runaway. Nia patted its great tin leg, and it did not frighten her at all. When the doors opened, the morning children rode giggling in a slow circle around it. The elephant carried them as gently as could be. Of everything she had done that night, Nia was proudest of this. She had taken the frightening thing and made it gentle. It was a heart-warming end to her long and brave adventure.

Keeper Fettle's letter had hinted at a deeper secret, hidden somewhere in the museum. Now Nia burned to know what it was. She looked about the warm little workshop for where it might lie. On the bench sat a thick private journal, full of Fettle's own words. In one corner, a little museum mouse kept darting out and looking back at her. It plainly wanted to lead her somewhere. And against the far wall stood a single locked cabinet, old and beautiful, the only thing in the room shut fast. Nia wondered which held the keeper's secret, and where she should look first.

Nia chose the journal. It was thick and worn, its leather cover soft with years of handling, and when she opened it a faint smell of oil and old paper rose from the pages. She settled herself on the workshop stool and turned the leaves slowly by the light of her candle stub. Tock curled up warm against her arm, tucking his little nose under, as content as she was to sit and read. The writing was small and neat and kind. Nia began at the beginning, and soon she was lost in Keeper Fettle's long, gentle story, page by page.

Page by page, Nia read how Keeper Fettle had built the whole menagerie himself. He had made it animal by animal, over a long and lonely lifetime of loving work. He made the brass larks first, when he was young. Then came the lions, the whales, and the butterflies, each one taking months of patient care. He had no family, the journal said, and few friends. The clockwork creatures were his company, his children almost. Nia's chest went tight to read it. All these hundreds of animals were one lonely old man's life, poured out in brass and tin. She turned the pages tenderly and read on.

At last Nia came to the very last entry, written in a shakier hand than the rest. In it, Keeper Fettle set down his dearest wish. He was growing old, he wrote, and he could not tend the animals forever. His one great hope was that someday a child would come, a child who cared enough to keep the menagerie alive after he was gone, so that his life's work would not fall silent and still. Nia read the words twice, and her eyes stung. It was as if he had written them for her alone. Softly, she closed the heavy journal.
