Nia closed the journal, understanding everything now. The whole menagerie was one old man's life, poured lovingly into metal, every brass feather and tin whisker of it. His greatest hope had been simply a child like her, come to care for it after him. So she found a scrap of paper and left him a note. She promised she would come back. She promised the animals would not be forgotten. And when Keeper Fettle read it later, he knew his life's work was safe in kind young hands at last. It was a tender ending, and a meaningful one, and Nia felt it change her a little.

A second little clockwork mouse, near twin to Tock, kept darting out ahead of Nia and then stopping to look back at her, its whiskers twitching. It plainly wanted her to follow it somewhere. Tock watched his small cousin with great interest from her shoulder. Nia hesitated. She could go chasing after the mouse into the dark corners of the workshop, to see wherever it was so eager to lead her. Or she could stay still and kneel down and coax it gently to come to her instead, and let it settle in her hand. She thought about which was wiser.

choices.label: Chase the mouse into the dark.
Coax the mouse gently to you.

nodes.body: Nia followed the little mouse into the dark. It scurried ahead of her, always just in reach of the candlelight. It led her round the workshop and along the skirting board. At last it stopped at a tiny door she would never have found alone. She knelt and peered inside. There, in a warm little hollow behind the wall, slept a whole nest of clockwork mice. Dozens of them lay curled up small and still. This was Keeper Fettle's secret, she realized, his own hidden family of tiny helpers. Nia's face lit up with wonder. She reached in gently to begin winding the little mice awake.

The little mouse had led Nia straight to Keeper Fettle's secret. It was a hidden nest of tiny clockwork mice, made long ago for company in his lonely work. One by one she wound them gently awake. One by one they stretched and blinked and scurried out, a dozen small helpers ready to tend the sleeping museum. When Fettle heard of it later, he was overjoyed. Someone had found his little friends at last. And Tock, proudest of all, suddenly had a whole family of his own. It was a sweet and surprising ending, and it made everyone glad.

The little mouse would not be caught. However gently Nia coaxed it, it only sat and watched her, whiskers twitching, keeping just out of reach. So in the end she gave up trying and simply let it stay, and it kept her company through the last dark hours of the night. She never did learn quite where it had wanted to lead her, or what secret it might have shown. But she was not alone, and that was something. When dawn came, it found the two of them, the girl and the little mouse, waiting quietly together for the keepers to arrive.

The one locked cabinet in the workshop was old and beautiful. Its dark wood was carved with leaves, and its little brass lock was worn smooth. Nia was sure it held Keeper Fettle's greatest secret of all. She studied it. On the master ring in her hand hung a whole set of tiny keys. She could try them one by one, until she found the one that fit. Or she could take up a workshop tool and force the little lock open, quick and rough. Nia looked at the delicate old cabinet. Then she thought carefully about which way to try.

choices.label: Try the small keys one by one.
Force the lock with a tool.

nodes.body: Nia chose to be patient. She held the ring of tiny keys up to the candlelight and began to try them in the little lock, one after another, gently, never forcing. The first did not fit. Nor did the second, nor the third. She did not grow cross or hurried; she simply worked her way along the ring, giving each key its fair turn. And at last, near the end of them, one small key slid in and turned with a soft, clean click. The cabinet door swung slowly open before her. Nia smiled. Patience had done what force never could, and done no harm at all.

Nia leaned in and looked inside the cabinet, and her breath caught. There, alone on a little velvet stand, stood the very first clockwork animal Fettle ever made. It was a small brass rabbit, worn smooth and pale with age. It was plainly older than every other creature in the whole museum. Beside it a faded note was pinned, in Fettle's own hand. In careful letters it said that this rabbit must always be kept safe, for it was the beginning of everything. Nia gazed at the small worn thing with tender care. Gently, she reached in to wind the little rabbit.

The cabinet had opened on Keeper Fettle's first creation. It was a little brass rabbit, worn smooth with a lifetime of love, the very seed of the whole great menagerie. Nia wound it with the greatest care, turning the key by the smallest amounts. The old rabbit began to hop, slow and faithful. It must have hopped just so a lifetime ago, when Fettle was young. Later the keeper heard that his first friend lived and hopped again. He could hardly believe it. He called Nia the finest thing that had ever happened to his museum. It was a precious, moving ending.

Nia should have been patient. Instead she forced the little lock with a tool. The delicate old catch was no match for it. With a small sad snap it broke, and the cabinet jammed itself tight shut. Now it would not open at all. Nia stared at it, her cheeks going hot. Nothing was harmed that a clever keeper could not one day mend. But she would never see the secret inside, not tonight. She felt rather foolish about it. There was nothing to do but wait, a little sheepish, to tell Keeper Fettle in the morning that she had been too impatient.

Perhaps the bravest thing of all, Nia thought, was simply to keep the sleeping museum safe and let the quiet dawn come in its own good time. She did not have to wake anything at all. But even so, there were still small choices to make. She could let all the animals sleep on and simply wait with them for the morning. She could wind just one single favourite creature to greet the dawn before the light grew strong. Or she could wait by the great front door to meet whoever came in first. Nia stood quiet in the grey light and thought about which felt right.

choices.label: Let them sleep and wait for morning.
Wait by the door to meet someone.

nodes.body: Nia chose to let them sleep. She settled down on the great spring-room floor, her back against a cool machine. She gathered Tock into her lap, where he ticked softly and warm. Together they watched the grey light of dawn climb slowly up the tall windows. It spilled little by little across the sleeping animals all around them. It was peaceful there in the hush. She did not feel she had to fix anything or wake anything. It was enough, just now, to keep them safe and watch the morning come. Nia leaned her head back and waited quietly for the dawn.

Nia kept the whole menagerie safe through the night. She let the dawn come gently, the animals still and sleeping, everything left exactly as the keeper had it. She had not fixed the great broken spring. No bird sang, and no beast stirred. But nothing had come to any harm, and that was worth a great deal. When the doors opened in the morning, she could tell the keepers all she had learned. She would tell of the broken spring, the hidden workshop, and the way to mend it. It was a safe and quiet ending, unfinished but kind, and Nia did not mind it at all.

Nia thought of all the animals she had met on her long night's wander. She picked the one that had touched her heart the most. There was no time to wake more than one, not with the sky already paling in the windows. So she took up the master key and hurried through the grey half-light. Her footsteps were soft on the worn floor. Tock rode along on her shoulder, ticking with excitement. Just one, she told herself, one little wonder to greet the morning. She reached her favourite and got ready to wind it awake.

Nia fitted the master key with great care, making sure it was seated true before she turned it. Then she began to wind, but only a little, only just enough to wake the creature for the morning. She did not wind it full. The rest of the wind she would save, leaving plenty for the keepers to finish in their own good time. That felt right to her, to take only what the morning needed and no more. Tock watched from her shoulder as she worked. Slowly the spring drew tight beneath her hands, and she felt the little life beginning to gather inside.

Under Nia's careful hands the animal began to stir to gentle life. First a small movement, then a tremble, then a slow and easy waking. There was nothing sudden or startling about it. She drew the master key back out and stepped softly away to watch. She hardly breathed, her whole face alight. It was a lovely thing to see, that one creature waking in the quiet while all the others slept on. Tock ticked happily beside her. Nia was so glad she had chosen this one. She waited, smiling, to greet the coming dawn with it.

Nia woke one single favourite animal to greet the dawn. All the rest slept on, safe and ready and waiting. When the children came in the morning, they found that one wonderful moving creature among all the still ones. It was a bright promise of wonders to come, once Keeper Fettle mended the rest. It stirred and moved just for them, gentle and alone in the hush. Nia treasured the quiet magic of her one small waking. It was dearer somehow for being only one. It was a lovely, hopeful ending, and it warmed her all the way home.

Nia went and waited by the great front door as the light slowly grew, watching the grey world brighten beyond the glass. Someone would come soon, she knew, and she thought about how best to meet them. She could simply wait where she was and watch for the museum keepers to arrive, and tell them everything face to face. She could leave a helpful note pinned up where they would be sure to see it. Or she could tuck herself out of sight for a moment first, until she understood just who was coming through the door. Nia weighed the three ways in her mind.

choices.label: Leave a helpful note for the morning.
Hide until you know who it is.

nodes.body: Nia chose to leave a note. She found a keeper's pad and a stub of pencil. Then she wrote out everything she had learned, plainly and clearly, so anyone could follow it. She wrote about the great broken spring. She wrote about the shining spare one, waiting in its place. And she wrote every step it would take to mend the two, just as Fettle's drawings had shown her. Then she pinned the note up where the morning staff could not miss it, right by the door. Tock watched her work, head tilted. She read it over once more, and nodded, satisfied.

Beside her clear note, Nia propped the master key. Whoever came would find it first thing, ready to hand. Now they would have both the instructions and the key together. Everything needed to wake the menagerie was in one place. She stepped back and looked at her tidy work with quiet pride. Then she found a spot nearby to wait. She was close enough to explain things herself, if the keepers had any questions, but out of the way until they came. Tock settled on her shoulder. The light grew brighter at the windows, and Nia waited calmly for morning.

The note Nia left told the morning keepers exactly how to mend the great broken spring, step by clear step. The master key stood propped ready beside it. So the moment they arrived, they set to work. By opening time they had the whole menagerie wound and moving. The children who poured in never knew how close the museum came to a silent morning. But Nia knew. And the grateful keepers knew too. They said her clear thinking had saved the whole morning. It was a clever and satisfying ending, and Nia went home tired and proud.

Nia waited quietly by the door. At dawn she met the first keeper who came through it, an older woman with kind eyes and a ring of keys. Nia told her the whole long story. She told of the broken spring, the hidden workshop, the mouse, the journal, and the night among the sleeping animals. The keeper listened, amazed. She thanked Nia warmly and promised to see her safely home. Nia had not mended the menagerie herself, it was true. But she left knowing she had truly helped, and that felt good. It was a calm and friendly ending to a long and wondrous night.

Nia chose to hide until she knew who was coming. She tucked herself behind a pillar as the door opened. But it was only the early cleaner, come to sweep before the crowds. He did not know a child was there at all. Humming to himself, he locked the inner gate behind him as he passed through. Now Nia was shut in the hall a while longer. She was quite safe, and it would all be sorted out before long. But her chance to explain the broken spring slipped quietly by, and the menagerie slept on. It was a small and gentle setback, no more.
